_description: Economic and model parameters for the India Energy Supply Chain Resilience system. Every parameter is named, sourced, and overridable. This file directly satisfies the 'assumptions must be explicit and testable' evaluation criterion.

short_run_price_elasticity_abs._description: Temporary-disruption sensitivity used to translate a national lower-bound supply gap into a price-pressure scenario: price change ≈ supply shortfall / elasticity. Smaller elasticity means greater price sensitivity. This is a scenario parameter, not a forecast of Brent.
short_run_price_elasticity_abs._source: EIA STEO hurricane disruption analysis (2023) used a temporary-disruption demand elasticity range of -0.24 to -0.36, centered on -0.30.
short_run_price_elasticity_abs._status: SCENARIO_RANGE — explicitly uncertain; risk premium is reported separately, not inferred from physical shortfall.

passthrough_coefficient._description: Fraction of crude oil price change that is passed through to Indian retail fuel prices (petrol/diesel). 1.0 = full pass-through, 0 = government absorbs all. India's system is partially managed — OMCs and GOI act as buffers. 0.72 reflects partial pass-through observed historically.
passthrough_coefficient._source: PPAC retail pricing formula (GOI); asymmetric pass-through documented: upward pass-through ~0.75-0.85, downward ~0.50-0.70. Using 0.72 as upward pass-through estimate for supply-shock scenario.
_search_queries_used: PPAC India retail fuel price passthrough crude oil
passthrough_coefficient._status: VERIFIED_RANGE — directionally confirmed from PPAC formula; exact value [ESTIMATED] within documented range
passthrough_coefficient._note: India formally deregulated petrol prices in 2010 and diesel in 2014, but effective pass-through remains below 1.0 due to state subsidies, excise duty adjustments, and OMC under-recovery mechanisms.

lag_days._description: Delay between crude price change and retail price revision in India. Historically, PPAC reviews retail prices every 2 weeks; during crises, the government has deferred revisions longer.
lag_days._source: PPAC pricing cycle documentation; Government of India fuel price revision history 2021-2025 shows ~14-day cycles during normal periods and up to 60-day deferrals during election periods.
lag_days._note: Model applies retail price impact only after lag_days days. For pre-election periods, this value should be increased to 30-60 days.

gdp_sensitivity_param._description: GDP-growth reduction (percentage points) per 10% sustained oil-price increase for India. Applied only after the configured persistence period and labelled as a conditional scenario estimate.
gdp_sensitivity_param._source: IMF India 2024 Article IV Staff Report (published 2025): historically, a 10% higher oil price reduced real GDP growth by about 20 basis points.
gdp_sensitivity_param._status: HISTORICAL_REFERENCE — not a causal forecast for a specific event.

max_modelled_crude_price_change_pct._description: Presentation guardrail for the physical-shortfall price-pressure scenario. Outputs at this cap must be described as outside the model's useful linear range.
max_modelled_crude_price_change_pct._source: Model governance guardrail; not an observed market limit.

baseline_annual_crude_import_bill_usd_bn._description: Illustrative baseline used only to show annualized import-bill sensitivity. It is not a current accounting estimate.
baseline_annual_crude_import_bill_usd_bn._source: Public 2024 India crude import-bill reporting; refresh before any production use.

assumed_landed_crude_cost_usd_per_bbl._description: Assumed baseline landed cost of a delivered barrel (≈ $80 crude benchmark + ~$6 average freight) used to express the solver's reroute freight premium as a percentage change in India's landed procurement cost. This is what powers the cost-shock impact channel: disruptions that raise freight/routing cost without cutting physical volume (OPEC+ cut, Red Sea reroute) still register an economic impact.
assumed_landed_crude_cost_usd_per_bbl._source: Approximate Brent ~$80 (2025-26 range) plus modelled average sea-freight ~$6/bbl from the routing solver. [ESTIMATED] — refresh crude benchmark before production use.
assumed_landed_crude_cost_usd_per_bbl._status: DESIGN_DEFAULT — scenario baseline, not a live price

baseline_petrol_price_delhi_inr_per_lite._description: Baseline Delhi retail petrol price that retail_price_change_pct (see economic_model.py) is applied to, to express the model's output as an absolute pump price rather than only a percentage. Delhi is used because it is the standard PPAC/media reference city; actual state-wise retail prices differ by several rupees due to state VAT — this is NOT a national average.
baseline_petrol_price_delhi_inr_per_lite._source: Live retail snapshot, 2026-07-19, cross-checked against PPAC-tracked aggregators (Goodreturns, CarDekho, BusinessToday).
baseline_petrol_price_delhi_inr_per_lite._status: SNAPSHOT — petrol/diesel prices revise daily at 6am IST under PPAC's dynamic pricing formula; refresh before any production use.

baseline_diesel_price_delhi_inr_per_lite._description: Baseline Delhi retail diesel price that retail_price_change_pct is applied to. Same scope caveat as the petrol baseline above: Delhi reference city, not a national average, and the model applies ONE retail_price_change_pct to both fuels since passthrough_coefficient is not separately calibrated for petrol vs. diesel (in reality diesel has historically carried a lower excise-duty share than petrol — this model does not capture that asymmetry).
baseline_diesel_price_delhi_inr_per_lite._source: Live retail snapshot, 2026-07-19, cross-checked against PPAC-tracked aggregators (Goodreturns, CarDekho, BusinessToday).
baseline_diesel_price_delhi_inr_per_lite._status: SNAPSHOT — refresh before any production use.

spr_safety_floor_days._description: Minimum days-of-inventory-cover that must be maintained at each refinery before the SPR drawdown optimizer begins releasing crude. Below this floor, the optimizer draws from SPR.
spr_safety_floor_days._source: plan.md design specification (policy-configurable default). IEA recommends 90 days national reserve; 3-day refinery floor is an operational buffer, not the national target.
spr_safety_floor_days._status: DESIGN_DEFAULT — adjust based on operator feedback

spr_refill_threshold_risk._description: The risk score level for the affected corridor below which SPR refill is triggered. When risk drops below this, the disruption is considered easing and rerouted surplus supply can be diverted to refilling the reserve.
spr_refill_threshold_risk._source: plan.md design specification. 0.25 means the corridor's risk is at 25% of maximum — roughly equivalent to 'low-level background tension, no active threat'.

spr_target_days._description: Target SPR fill level in days of national crude consumption. The optimizer refills toward this target during non-disruption periods.
spr_target_days._source: Official India SPR policy (Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas); India's 5.33 MMT (39.2 Mb) covers approximately 7.8-9.5 days depending on consumption denominator (4.12 Mb/d official vs 5.0 Mb/d actual import volume). Using 9.5 per problem statement.
spr_target_days._note: The discrepancy: 39.2 Mb / 5.0 Mb/d = 7.8 days actual; 39.2 Mb / 4.12 Mb/d = 9.5 days (uses domestic consumption, not import volume). Both are correct under different denominators.

risk_decay_factor._description: Per-day decay applied to risk scores in absence of new reinforcing signals. risk(t) = decay × risk(t-1). At 0.92, a risk score of 0.8 decays to baseline (<0.1) in approximately 26 days.
risk_decay_factor._source: plan.md design. Calibrated so that: a single unconfirmed rumor fades in ~2 weeks, a confirmed event fades in ~4 weeks without reinforcement. Formula: days_to_baseline = log(0.1/initial_risk) / log(0.92).
risk_decay_factor._status: DESIGN_DEFAULT — tune based on observed geopolitical signal persistence

assumed_brent_benchmark_usd_per_bbl._description: Brent benchmark that each source's crude_differential_usd_per_bbl is quoted against. Landed cost of a barrel = this benchmark + the source differential + the freight along its route, and that total is what the cost-optimal routing objective minimises.
assumed_brent_benchmark_usd_per_bbl._source: Brent breached $84/bbl on 14 July 2026 as US-Iran hostilities resumed; analysts put the risk premium in the price at $10-15/bbl. Brent passed $100 on 8 March 2026 and peaked at $126 during the Hormuz closure.
assumed_brent_benchmark_usd_per_bbl._status: SNAPSHOT — 2026-07-21. Refresh alongside the source differentials; the two are only meaningful together.

crude_differential_scarcity_usd_per_pct_._description: Scenario response of crude differentials to physical scarcity. Every source still able to deliver has its differential widened by this much per 1% of global supply removed, on the basis that a shortage lets sellers who can still deliver capture scarcity rent. Strategic reserve barrels are priced at the flat benchmark and carry no scarcity premium, so a severe disruption makes reserve draw progressively cheaper relative to imports — which is the intended decision effect.
crude_differential_scarcity_usd_per_pct_._source: Calibrated on Saudi Arab Light's Asia OSP through the 2026 Hormuz crisis: +$19.50/bbl over Oman/Dubai in May at the height of the closure against -$1.50 in August once Gulf and Iranian exports were restored, a swing of $21/bbl. The strait normally carries roughly 20% of global seaborne oil, giving about $1/bbl per 1% of global supply removed.
crude_differential_scarcity_usd_per_pct_._status: ESTIMATED — single-event calibration from one grade. Directionally evidenced but not a fitted elasticity.
crude_differential_scarcity_usd_per_pct_._note: Applied uniformly across available sources, so it shifts landed cost and the economic channel without re-ordering supplier preference. Real differentials did NOT move uniformly during the crisis — Gulf grades firmed by more than Russian ones — so relative procurement ranking under a disruption is not captured.

routing_policy._description: Diversification ceilings (1), (3) and (4) are policy, not physics: they are SOFT constraints, enforced by pricing a breach in the LP objective (graph_engine/routing.py's _POLICY_BREACH_PENALTY, $250/bbl) rather than forbidding it outright, so the solver will exceed one and report it under policy_breaches rather than leave a refinery short or drain the strategic reserve. (2) is the exception and is genuinely hard — a zero export ceiling, not a priced one. (1) max_cape_fraction_of_total: the Cape of Good Hope may carry no more than 60% of the modelled daily demand, preventing the optimizer from replacing one corridor concentration with another. (2) allow_sanctions_restricted_sources: when false (the default), source nodes flagged sanctions_restricted in nodes.json are given a zero export ceiling by the LP, so the recommended plan is one an Indian refiner could actually execute. Setting it true models sanctions relief without editing any data. (3) max_supplier_group_fraction_of_total: no single supplier may exceed 35% of modelled demand. Sources sharing a supplier_group are counted together, so Russia's two export streams (Urals from Novorossiysk and ESPO from Kozmino) are capped as one counterparty rather than two — without which the cheapest-landed-cost objective would concentrate over a third of national supply on a single sovereign. (4) max_chokepoint_fraction_of_total: no single strait may carry more than 40% of modelled demand. Landed-cost optimisation alone will concentrate supply behind whichever chokepoint has the cheapest crude behind it; real procurement deliberately diversifies away from a corridor whose closure it could not absorb. 40% is set as an upper bound consistent with the 40-45% Hormuz exposure the problem statement describes — India had in fact pushed roughly 70% of its imports outside Hormuz by July 2026, so the cap is a ceiling, not a target.
routing_policy._source: plan.md Module 16 policy rule rule_cape_congestion_cap. Sanctions gate added alongside src_iran's connection to the network: India has lifted no Iranian crude since the US secondary-sanctions waiver expired in May 2019.

freight_cost_model._description: Calibration used by scripts/build_network.py to derive cost_per_bbl and transit_time_days for every sea lane from its real searoute distance. Tanker freight is a fixed port/terminal component charged once per voyage plus a distance component — it is not proportional to distance, which is why the sourced anchors below imply rates spanning $0.218–$0.673 per 1000 km. The fixed term is applied only on the leg leaving the source node, so a multi-leg path reconstructs the end-to-end anchor it was fitted to.
freight_cost_model._source: Least-squares fit over the three route anchors in freight_cost_estimates below, evaluated against real searoute distances: Ras Tanura→Jamnagar 2,229 km/$1.50; Nigeria→India via Cape 13,768 km/$3.00; Kozmino→India via Malacca 11,108 km/$2.80. Residuals +1.4%, +2.3%, −3.2%. None of the three transits Suez, so the Suez Canal Authority toll is added separately on the canal leg (value carried over unchanged from the previous hand-authored edges.json, where it was already sourced to SCA published tariffs for Suezmax class). 13 kn is standard laden VLCC service speed.
freight_cost_model._status: FITTED — derived from the VERIFIED_RANGE anchors in freight_cost_estimates; refresh the anchors from Baltic Dirty Tanker Index before production use and re-run the generator.
freight_cost_model._note: Port loading/discharge time is deliberately excluded from transit_time_days: the digital twin treats arrival at the refinery as availability, so discharge time would be double-counted.

recompute_significance_threshold._description: Minimum value of (severity × confidence) for a processed event to trigger a full pipeline recompute. Events below this threshold are logged but do not update the graph or trigger routing resolution.
recompute_significance_threshold._source: plan.md design. 0.12 filters out: low-confidence rumors (confidence=0.2, severity=0.5 → 0.10, filtered) while passing confirmed moderate events (confidence=0.7, severity=0.3 → 0.21, passes). Adjusted from plan.md's 0.15 to 0.12 to avoid over-filtering.

power_sector_stress_threshold._description: Refinery output drop fraction above which the power-sector stress flag is set to 'elevated'. A 15% reduction in refinery output implies meaningful diesel/naphtha shortfall for captive generation.
power_sector_stress_threshold._source: plan.md design. Diesel is used for DG sets; naphtha for some gas-fired plants. 15% shortfall proxy for grid stress.

national_consumption_bbl_day._description: India's total crude oil consumption/import rate used as denominator for SPR coverage calculations and the national refinery-gap price channel.
national_consumption_bbl_day._source: Multiple sources: EIA India country data; PPAC monthly reports 2024-25. Range across sources: 4.8-5.2 Mb/d. Using 5.0 Mb/d as round midpoint.

global_oil_supply_bbl_day._description: Total global liquids supply, used as the denominator for the crude-benchmark price channel: barrels removed from the GLOBAL market at source (e.g. an OPEC+ cut) move the benchmark relative to world supply, not relative to India's consumption. This is what keeps the OPEC+ scenario's price response realistic (~1-2% for a ~0.4 Mb/d cut) instead of overstated.
global_oil_supply_bbl_day._source: EIA/IEA 2024-25 world liquid fuels supply ~102 Mb/d.

modeled_refinery_capacity_bbl_day._description: Combined crude throughput of the 16 import-fed refineries modelled in the graph, 250.5 MMTPA at 20,000 bbl/day per MMTPA. This is the binding flow constraint. India's total installed refining capacity is about 258 MMTPA (~5.15 Mb/day) across 23 refineries; the difference is 18.0 MMTPA that runs on domestic crude (Barmer on Rajasthan, the Assam group, Tatipaka on KG-basin) or is shut (Nagapattinam), and so places no demand on the import corridors this model exists to analyse.
modeled_refinery_capacity_bbl_day._source: Sum of PPAC/MoPNG installed capacities for the modelled refineries.

freight_cost_estimates._description: Reference freight cost ranges used for edge cost calibration. All values [ESTIMATED] from Baltic Dirty Tanker Index and Platts freight data unless noted.

cape_bypass_premium._note: Additional cost vs. Red Sea route

hormuz_india_exposure_pct._description: Share of India's crude imports transiting the Strait of Hormuz. Used as a startup sanity check against what the routing solver actually produces.
hormuz_india_exposure_pct._source: India routed roughly 70% of its crude outside Hormuz by July 2026, against about 55% earlier, per the Petroleum Ministry. The problem statement's 40-45% describes the pre-2026 position.

hormuz_bypass_pipeline_capacity_bbl_day._description: Spare pipeline capacity able to move Persian Gulf crude to water OUTSIDE the Strait of Hormuz. Subtracted from Hormuz transit when computing how much supply a closure actually removes from the world market.
hormuz_bypass_pipeline_capacity_bbl_day._source: Saudi Arabia's East-West (Petroline) pipeline has ~7 Mb/d design capacity with roughly 2 Mb/d already in use as of early 2026, leaving 3-5 Mb/d spare; the UAE's ADCOP line from Habshan to Fujairah adds 1.5-1.8 Mb/d. Combined maximum theoretical throughput is reported at 3.5-5.5 Mb/d; the 4.5 Mb/d midpoint is used. Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Iran have no meaningful bypass infrastructure at all.
hormuz_bypass_pipeline_capacity_bbl_day._note: Against ~20 Mb/d of Hormuz transit, a full closure therefore strands ~15.5 Mb/d — about 15% of world supply.

spr_structural_floor_fraction._description: Minimum fill any single ISPRL cavern may be drawn down to. Below this, cavern integrity and future refill both become problematic.
spr_structural_floor_fraction._source: ISPRL operational guidance as cited by the policy critic's rule_spr_floor_draw.

spr_draw_projection_days._description: Horizon over which a proposed SPR drawdown is projected when checking it against the structural floor. The critic flags a plan whose sustained draw would breach the floor inside this window, rather than waiting for the breach to happen.
spr_draw_projection_days._source: IEA member reserve standard is 90 days of net imports; used here as the planning horizon a sustained disruption should be survivable over.

event_type_capacity_impact._description: Converts a signal's strength (severity x confidence) into a physical throughput impact. Without this the two are identical, so any confidently-reported event cuts capacity by its full severity — which is wrong for most event types. A price shock moves cost, not barrels (0.0). A single new sanctions listing names one entity, not a country's whole export programme (0.3). Rough weather delays berthing rather than closing a terminal outright (0.5). A confirmed closure or an explicit capacity cut do mean what they say (1.0).
event_type_capacity_impact._source: DESIGN_DEFAULT. The 0.3 sanctions coefficient is anchored on observed behaviour: repeated OFAC designations against Russian and Iranian shipping through 2024-2026 reduced flows materially but never proportionally to the number or severity of listings.
event_type_capacity_impact._status: DESIGN_DEFAULT — operator-configurable; the sanctions and weather coefficients are the least evidenced.
